title: Global vs. Local Receptive Fields
concept: CNNs build receptive fields locally and expand them gradually, whereas ViTs have a global receptive field from the very first layer.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main complexity limitation of standard self-attention in ViT?

options.text: It scales quadratically (O(N²)) with the sequence length.
It requires recurrent loops that cannot be parallelized.
It only works on monochrome images.

exercises.explain: Correct! dot-product self-attention scales quadratically, making high resolutions computationally heavy.

cards.title: The bird's eye view
cards.body: If you look at a painting from one inch away, you only see a single brush stroke. You must step back to see the whole image. CNNs start one inch away and slowly step back layer-by-step. ViTs start with a bird's eye view, seeing the entire canvas in their very first step.

cards.title: Receptive Fields
cards.body: The receptive field is the region of the input image that influences a particular feature activation. In CNNs, the receptive field is restricted by the kernel size (local) and only grows as you stack more layers. In ViTs, self-attention allows a token to interact with every other token in the image immediately (global receptive field).

cards.title: Telephone game vs. group chat
cards.body: A CNN is like a game of telephone: information is passed from neighbor to neighbor, slowly spreading across the room. A ViT is like a group chat: anyone can send a message that everyone reads instantly, regardless of where they are sitting.

cards.title: Looking across the frame
cards.body: In a CNN, a neuron in layer 2 can only compare pixels within a 5x5 area. In a ViT, a token in block 1 representing a dog's head in the top-left can compute attention scores with the dog's tail in the bottom-right immediately.

cards.title: Global isn't always best
cards.body: You might think a global view is always superior. However, in early layers, local details (like edges and textures) are often more important than global relations. Because ViTs lack a local constraint, they can waste compute looking for relation patterns that don't exist.

cards.title: Local vs. Global Receptive Fields
cards.body: How information flows in early layers:

left.text:
Receptive field: Local (e.g., 3x3)
Extracts sharp local features
No global context yet

right.text:
Receptive field: Global (image-wide)
Extracts relationships immediately
Higher memory footprint

exercises.prompt: How does the receptive field of a ViT differ from a standard CNN in early layers?
exercises.explain: Self-attention allows ViT tokens to query all other tokens in the first layer, granting an immediate global receptive field.

options.text: ViT has a global receptive field immediately, while CNN is restricted to local kernels
CNN has a global receptive field immediately, while ViT only looks at the center patch
Both have identical receptive fields at all depths
ViTs have no receptive field because they do not use pixels

exercises.prompt: True or false: A CNN's receptive field gets larger as you go deeper into the network.
exercises.explain: True. As layers are stacked, neurons pool info from larger regions of the previous layer, expanding the receptive field.
Self-attention enables immediate global communication across all token positions.
exercises.sentence: While CNNs rely on local sliding kernels, ViTs use ___ to establish global connections in early layers.

exercises.prompt: Which of these visual tasks benefits most from an immediate global receptive field?
exercises.explain: Connecting distant components across the frame requires global context, which ViTs extract in one step.

options.text: Determining if a hand in the bottom-left is holding a cup in the top-right of a portrait
Finding a sharp black horizontal edge in a close-up texture
Smoothing out pixel noise in a blurry background

exercises.prompt: If early layers of ViT have global attention, do they still learn to focus on local features?

options.text: Yes — during training, the model learns to set high attention weights on adjacent patches, effectively 'learning' local fields where needed.
No — they are mathematically blocked from focusing on close patches.
Yes — but only if we delete the position embeddings.
No — they only look at the border patches of the image.

exercises.explain: Phenomenal! Because ViT is flexible, it learns to specialize some attention heads to focus locally, mimicking convolutions, while others look globally. Next: robustness and interpretability!
